title: Monotonic clock-domain gate for Event Timing evidence

primary_sources.relevance: Defines performance.now() and performance.timeOrigin on a stable monotonic clock and warns that Date.now() is subject to clock skew and adjustment.
Defines PerformanceEntry timestamps relative to a performance time origin.

finding: Freshness and chronology decisions for same-navigation performance evidence should use one monotonic clock domain, not wall-clock timestamps. A navigation token alone does not detect a packet copied from a different performance time origin.
design_implication: Collect performance.timeOrigin and performance.now() internally, compare capability and replay evidence in the same navigation, reject origin mismatch, reversed chronology, and monotonic staleness, then emit only a privacy-bounded decision packet without raw clock values.

observed: The W3C High Resolution Time draft states that Date.now() can be affected by clock skew and adjustments.
Seven deterministic tests passed in an isolated Node runtime.
inferred: Wall-clock freshness can misclassify evidence when the system clock changes between collection and evaluation.
proposed: Require clock_binding_valid before the R-015 integration result can authorize replay interpretation.
unresolved: No physical iPhone Safari packet has been evaluated.
The production freshness budget is not empirically calibrated.
No deployed Vercel path invokes this gate yet.

falsification_route: Run the gate with different time origins but identical navigation tokens and verify rejection.
Adjust the wall clock during a physical browser session and verify monotonic packet age remains ordered.
Compare accepted gate results against replay packets from a deliberately reloaded navigation.
